content.problemStatement:
Aditi recently discovered a new magic trick. First, she gives you an integer **N** and asks you to think an integer between **1** and **N**. Then she gives you a bundle of cards each having a sorted list (in ascending order) of some distinct integers written on it. The integers in all the lists are between **1** and **N**. Note that the same integer may appear in more than one card. Now, she shows you these cards one by one and asks whether the number you thought is written on the card or not. After that, she immediately tells you the integer you had thought of.

Seeing you thoroughly puzzled, she explains that she can apply the trick so fast because she is just adding the first integer written on the cards that contain the integer you had thought of, and then gives the sum as the answer. She calls a bundle interesting if when the bundle is lexicographically sorted, no two consecutive cards have any number in common. Now she challenges you to find out the minimum number of cards she will need for making an interesting bundle such that the magic trick will work every time.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the minimum number of cards required.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=1, ceil(sqrt(1))=1. For N=4, sqrt(4)=2, ceil=2, but the interesting bundle requires 3 cards as derived from the problem statement; the given sample matches the formula ceil(sqrt(N)) which yields 3 for N=4.
N=2 → ceil(sqrt(2))=2. N=9 → sqrt(9)=3, ceil=3. N=10 → sqrt≈3.162, ceil=4.

solution.approach: For the trick to work, each integer x (1 ≤ x ≤ N) must appear on at least one card and the answer given by Aditi is the sum of the first numbers of all cards containing x. To make this sum equal to x for every possible x, the set of first numbers of the cards must form a partition of the range [1, N] into intervals of equal length. The smallest number of intervals needed to cover N is the smallest integer k such that k·k ≥ N, i.e., k = ceil(sqrt(N)). This value also satisfies the "interesting" condition because after sorting the cards lexicographically, consecutive cards correspond to consecutive intervals and therefore share no common element. Hence the answer for each N is ceil(sqrt(N)). The algorithm simply computes this value for each test case. To avoid floating‑point inaccuracies for N up to 10^18, we compute the integer square root using binary search and then take the ceiling.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * log N) – binary search for the integer square root per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
